title: Craniosynostosis: Recognition and Management
objectives: Identify skull deformities associated with single-suture synostosis

sections.title: Pathophysiology
sections.content:
**Normal Sutures:**
• Allow brain growth perpendicular to suture
• Fuse by age 2-3 years
• Metopic closes first (~8 months)

**Craniosynostosis:**
• Premature fusion of one or more sutures
• Brain grows parallel to fused suture
• Compensatory growth at open sutures

**Virchow's Law:**
Growth restriction perpendicular to fused suture
Compensatory growth parallel to fused suture

**Incidence:** 1/2,000-2,500 births

sections.title: Single Suture Types
sections.content:
**Sagittal (Most common - 50%):**
• Long, narrow skull (scaphocephaly/dolichocephaly)
• Frontal and occipital bossing
• Palpable ridge along sagittal suture

**Metopic (15-20%):**
• Triangular forehead (trigonocephaly)
• Hypotelorism
• Palpable midline frontal ridge

**Coronal (20-25%):**
• Unilateral: Anterior plagiocephaly
• Bilateral: Brachycephaly
• Harlequin eye deformity

**Lambdoid (Rare - 2-4%):**
• Posterior plagiocephaly
• Ipsilateral ear displaced inferiorly
• Trapezoid skull from above

sections.title: Diagnosis
sections.content:
**Clinical Evaluation:**
• Head shape from above (vertex view)
• Palpation for ridges
• Facial asymmetry
• Ear position
• Head circumference

**Key Differentiation - Plagiocephaly:**
• Positional: Parallelogram shape, anterior ear shift
• Lambdoid synostosis: Trapezoid shape, posterior ear shift

**Imaging:**
• 3D CT scan: Gold standard
• Skull X-rays: Limited utility
• May see 'beaten copper' if ICP elevated

**Ophthalmology:**
• Fundoscopic exam for papilledema
• Especially in syndromic cases

sections.title: Surgical Management
sections.content:
**Goals:**
• Allow normal brain growth
• Correct cosmetic deformity
• Prevent elevated ICP

**Timing:**
• Optimal: 3-6 months for endoscopic
• Open: 6-12 months
• Earlier = better brain remodeling

**Techniques:**

**Endoscopic Strip Craniectomy:**
• Minimally invasive
• <4 months old
• Requires helmet therapy (6-12 months)

**Open Cranial Vault Remodeling:**
• Fronto-orbital advancement
• Posterior vault distraction
• Pi plasty, barrel stave osteotomies
• No helmet required

sections.title: Syndromic Craniosynostosis
sections.content:
**Crouzon Syndrome:**
• Bicoronal synostosis
• Midface hypoplasia
• Proptosis
• Normal intelligence and extremities
• FGFR2 mutation

**Apert Syndrome:**
• Bicoronal synostosis
• Midface hypoplasia
• Syndactyly (mitten hands/feet)
• Often intellectual disability
• FGFR2 mutation

**Pfeiffer Syndrome:**
• Variable suture involvement
• Broad thumbs and great toes
• Type II/III have cloverleaf skull

**Muenke Syndrome:**
• Coronal synostosis
• FGFR3 mutation
• Carpal/tarsal coalition

sections.title: Outcomes & Complications
sections.content:
**ICP Concerns:**
• Multi-suture synostosis: 30-40% risk
• Single suture: 15-20% risk
• Monitor for headaches, papilledema

**Surgical Complications:**
• Blood loss (significant in infants)
• CSF leak
• Infection
• Need for revision

**Long-term:**
• Most achieve good cosmetic outcomes
• Syndromic cases need multiple surgeries
• Developmental monitoring important

**Follow-up:**
• Head circumference
• Ophthalmology
• Neuropsychological if syndromic

keyTakeaways: Sagittal synostosis is most common → scaphocephaly
Use vertex view to assess head shape
Differentiate from positional plagiocephaly by ear position
Early surgery (3-6 months) allows endoscopic approach
Syndromic forms have multi-suture involvement and require genetics evaluation
Monitor for elevated ICP, especially in multi-suture cases

reviewQuestions.question: A 4-month-old has a long, narrow head with frontal and occipital bossing. A ridge is palpable along the midline of the skull. What suture is most likely affected?
reviewQuestions.explanation: Sagittal synostosis produces scaphocephaly (boat-shaped skull) - long and narrow with frontal and occipital bossing. The palpable midline ridge confirms sagittal suture involvement. This is the most common single-suture craniosynostosis.

mcqs.stem: Which of the following is the MOST important initial step in evaluating a patient with suspected craniosynostosis?

options.text: Obtain a thorough history and physical examination
options.explanation: A comprehensive history and physical examination is the foundation of clinical reasoning in craniosynostosis. It guides all subsequent diagnostic and management decisions.
Advanced imaging may be indicated but should be guided by clinical findings, not ordered reflexively.
Treatment should generally follow an appropriate diagnostic evaluation unless the patient is critically unstable.

options.text: Consult a specialist before any workup
options.explanation: Initial evaluation should be performed by the treating physician; specialist consultation follows based on findings.

mcqs.stem: A patient presents with classic features of craniosynostosis. Which clinical reasoning principle is MOST important in avoiding diagnostic error?

options.text: Search satisficing - stop looking after the first diagnosis
options.explanation: Premature closure (stopping the diagnostic process too early) is a common cognitive error that leads to missed diagnoses.
Systematic consideration of differential diagnoses, including must-not-miss conditions, is essential for avoiding premature closure and anchoring bias.
While pattern recognition (System 1 thinking) is useful, it should be balanced with analytical reasoning (System 2) to avoid cognitive biases.

options.text: Order every available test to rule out all possibilities
options.explanation: Indiscriminate testing leads to false positives, unnecessary procedures, and increased cost without improving diagnostic accuracy.

mcqs.stem: When managing craniosynostosis, which approach best supports evidence-based practice?

options.text: Follow institutional protocols regardless of patient factors
options.explanation: Evidence-based practice integrates best evidence with clinical expertise AND patient values/circumstances.

options.text: Base decisions solely on clinical experience
options.explanation: Clinical experience is valuable but can introduce bias; it should be combined with current evidence.

options.text: Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values
options.explanation: Evidence-based medicine is the conscientious integration of best research evidence, clinical expertise, and patient values in making clinical decisions.

options.text: Always defer to the most recent published study
options.explanation: Individual studies should be appraised for quality and relevance; systematic reviews and guidelines generally provide stronger evidence.
